goal: Reduce interruptions, finish more MITs, protect morning deep work, add exercise and side-project time, and stop working late.
constraints: End-of-day shutdown ritual must be scheduled as a calendar block and treated as non-negotiable.
Daily meeting hours should be limited to 2–3 hours to preserve deep-work blocks.
decisions: Batch similar tasks (calls and emails) to reduce cognitive switching and interruptions; group by channel/type and timebox into dedicated blocks.
Treat side-project evenings as non-negotiable calendar blocks with explicit timeboxing and start rituals.
Cancellation/reschedule rule for side-project sessions to prevent lost blocks.
Establish a recurring 'Shutdown — offline' calendar block and alarm to enforce the end-of-day ritual.

Adopt a hybrid night-before planning + brief morning anchor routine to reduce procrastination; implement 5–15 minute night planning plus 3–7 minute morning anchor with defined MITs and calendar scheduling.
Define and apply a daily meeting-hours cap (target 2–3 hours) and prioritize scheduling to cluster meetings and protect deep work.
entities: Batching reduces context switching and interruptions; create explicit check windows for batches.
Schedule side-project evenings as recurring Busy calendar blocks; prep checklist and start ritual.
End-of-day shutdown ritual: calendar block, alarm, and transition cue.

Night-before planning protocol: capture inbox items, select 1–3 MITs, write a single actionable first step for each, place first MIT on calendar with start time and duration; set a timer; prep materials; add an implementation intention.
Morning anchor protocol: quick hydration/movement, re-read MITs, confirm start time, start timer and begin the first focused block.
Hybrid planning approach to reduce procrastination.
Calendar retro-log (least friction): Use your normal calendar; create events after each major activity with Busy status.
Pomodoro / timer counts (minimal live tracking): Run Pomodoro cycles and log counts in a single note; include meetings and breaks.
Single-note timestamps (paper or note app): Keep a running note with timestamps at switches; track interruptions.
Passive app (if you don’t mind one tool): Use a start/stop tracker; start when you begin something, stop when you switch.
Simple categories to use: Deep Focus, Meetings / Calls, Email / Admin / Reactive, Breaks / Walk / Exercise, Interruptions, Other / Personal.
Quick 3-day plan: prep (5–10 min), days 1–3 log as you go (or retro-log hourly), day 4 review (sum time per category, compute percentages, count interruptions).

What to look for in the review: total deep-focus hours per day and % of total work time; interruptions count and timing; MITs started/completed; biggest time sinks; best/worst time-of-day for concentration.
A simple quick metric: if deep focus is under 2–3 hours/day, focus on protecting morning blocks and reducing reactive checks.
Meeting-hours cap: 2–3 hours per day; preference for clustering meetings to preserve focused blocks.
